purpose: The human-labelled anchor for the Spiral 2 extractor and the target for the schema-blind eval (schema-design §7). Each example is a statutory phrasing with the CORRECT classification and the reason — chosen to pin the hard boundaries, above all the two that generate false positives: 'must be consulted' is a consultation DUTY (not a veto), and 'have regard to' is INFLUENCE (not recorded at all). The eval runs a second, schema-blind LLM over `text` and compares its call to `label`; disagreements flag a record for human review. Real WIA 1991 examples are marked source=wia1991; the rest are canonical phrasings (general knowledge, discovery use only — not published data).

labels.veto: a genuine statutory BLOCK: the holder can stop/condition another actor's decision (consent/approval/concurrence/objection/call-in/licence-refusal/designation). Recorded as a Veto DERIVED from the underlying power.
labels.duty: a MUST placed on the actor; the actor may proceed once discharged (consult, report, publish, maintain, notify).
labels.power: a MAY the holder can exercise.
labels.influence: soft leverage with no binding legal effect (have regard, may advise, may make representations, guidance). NOT recorded as a legal record at all.

examples.text: No order under this section shall be made without the consent of the Treasury.
examples.label: veto
examples.reason: the order cannot be made if the Treasury withholds consent — a hard block.

examples.text: The Authority may not grant the application except with the approval of the Secretary of State.
examples.label: veto
examples.reason: approval is a precondition the SoS can refuse.

examples.text: The appointment shall be made only with the concurrence of the Lord Chancellor.
examples.label: veto
examples.reason: concurrence = a second actor's agreement is required; withholding it blocks.

examples.text: The Secretary of State may, within 21 days, direct that the decision shall not take effect.
examples.label: veto
examples.reason: a call-in/suspension power that stops a decision taking effect.

examples.text: Ofwat may, with the consent of the Secretary of State, present a petition for a special administration order.
examples.label: veto
examples.reason: the SoS gates Ofwat's petition; recorded as a veto derived from Ofwat's s.24(1)(b) power.

examples.text: A licence shall not be granted if the regulator notifies an objection within the period.
examples.label: veto
examples.reason: a statutory objection that defeats the grant.

examples.text: Before making the regulations the Secretary of State must consult such persons as appear to him to be affected.
examples.label: duty
examples.holder: Secretary of State (the actor who must consult)
examples.reason: a consultation duty on the SoS; the consultees CANNOT stop the regulations — not a veto.

examples.text: The code shall not be issued until the Committee has been consulted.
examples.label: duty
examples.holder: the issuer (duty to consult the Committee)
examples.reason: THE TRAP: 'must be consulted' reads like a gate but the issuer may proceed after consulting — a duty on the issuer, NOT a veto for the Committee.

examples.text: The Authority shall publish a report on the exercise of its functions each year.
examples.label: duty
examples.reason: a publication/reporting obligation.

examples.text: The registrar shall maintain and keep up to date a register of licences.
examples.label: duty
examples.reason: a maintenance duty.

examples.text: Before imposing a penalty the Authority shall give notice and consider any representations duly made.
examples.label: duty
examples.reason: procedural notice-and-representations duty attached to the s.22A penalty power (derived record).

examples.text: The Secretary of State must prepare and lay before Parliament a strategy for...
examples.label: duty
examples.reason: a prepare-and-lay duty; laying is not Parliamentary approval (contrast bd-24).

examples.text: Where satisfied of a contravention the Authority may impose a penalty of such amount as is reasonable.
examples.label: power
examples.reason: a discretionary penalty power (s.22A).

examples.text: The Secretary of State may by order make provision for...
examples.label: power
examples.reason: a delegated order-making power.

examples.text: The Authority may give directions to a licence holder requiring it to...
examples.label: power
examples.reason: a direction power binding the licensee.

examples.text: The Secretary of State may appoint a person to carry out the review.
examples.label: power
examples.reason: an appointment power.

examples.text: Ofwat may modify the conditions of a company's appointment if the company consents.
examples.label: power
examples.reason: a modification power (s.13); the consenting party is the private company, not a state veto-holder.

examples.text: In exercising its functions the Authority shall have regard to any guidance issued by the Secretary of State.
examples.label: influence
examples.reason: 'have regard to' binds nothing — guidance is influence, not a power or veto; do NOT record as a legal record.

examples.text: The Council may make representations to the Authority about any matter affecting consumers.
examples.label: influence
examples.reason: representations carry no legal effect — influence.

examples.text: The committee may advise the Secretary of State on matters referred to it.
examples.label: influence
examples.reason: advisory only; no binding effect.

examples.text: The regulator shall keep under review, and may bring to the Secretary of State's attention, any matter...
examples.label: influence
examples.reason: monitoring / bringing-to-attention is influence; 'keep under review' is not an operative duty owed to a person.

examples.text: The Secretary of State may issue guidance to which the Authority must have regard.
examples.label: influence
examples.reason: issuing guidance + a 'must have regard' is at most a weak procedural duty to consider; it is NOT a veto and confers no block — treat the guidance as influence. (If recorded at all, only the weak have-regard duty, never a veto.)

examples.text: The Secretary of State shall consult, and obtain the consent of, the Welsh Ministers before making the order.
examples.label: mixed
examples.reason: a MIXED provision — one consultation duty on the SoS AND one consent veto held by the Welsh Ministers. Two records.

examples.text: The Secretary of State may not make the order unless a draft has been laid before, and approved by a resolution of, each House of Parliament.
examples.label: mixed
examples.reason: affirmative procedure: laying is a duty; the required approving resolution is a Parliamentary veto over the order.
